title: India Summons US Diplomat Over Sailors Killed in Hormuz Strike; Congress Says Modi Went Soft at G7
summary: Three Indian nationals — deck cadet Aditya Sharma, engine fitter Shivanand Chaurasiya, and chief engineer Patnala Suresh — were killed when US forces struck the tanker MT Settebello on June 9. India formally protested by summoning the US chargé d'affaires and urged Washington to halt strikes on commercial shipping. At the G7 in Évian, Modi raised seafarer safety with Trump, who offered condolences but made no concrete commitment. Congress has accused the government of prioritising optics over accountability.

coverages.headline: Modi flags deaths of Indian seafarers in US strike, calls for secure sea routes at G7
coverages.excerpt: At Évian-les-Bains, PM Modi told G7 leaders including Trump that the security of seafarers is equally important and called for maritime trade routes to be kept safe; Trump expressed condolences but made no commitment.
coverages.bias_notes: The Federal's headline accurately captures the asymmetry here — Modi 'flags' and 'calls for', while Trump offers condolences without commitment. That gap between raising an issue diplomatically and securing any concrete response is the substantive story, and the piece reports it plainly rather than inflating Modi's G7 remarks into a diplomatic win. What's missing: any explanation of why the US has not acknowledged responsibility or offered compensation for killing the nationals of a partner country during a blockade operation — a question with real legal and accountability weight that diplomatic coverage tends to skip past.
